title: IT Purchasing: The View From the End of the Tunnel
author: Peter S. Kastner
source_file: 2002 IT Purchasing--The View From the End of the Tunnel PSK.pdf
abstract: An analysis of Top 20 IT company revenues in August 2002 suggests the IT spending recession may be at or near its trough. Aberdeen identifies significant directional changes in IT management that will become evident when spending recovers, including shifts toward EAI, Security, and CRM investment. The study warns that IT organizations and suppliers that ignore these structural changes will miss opportunities and lose market share when the recovery arrives.
importance_rationale: Published at the trough of the 2001-2002 IT recession, this study used Top 20 IT company revenue data to identify the spending floor — a valuable reference point for understanding the IT cycle. Aberdeen's identification of specific technology segments poised to benefit from recovery is a notable directional forecast.
relevance_rationale: The specific IT recession analysis is historical, but the methodology — using Top 20 IT vendor revenue trends as a leading indicator of spending recovery — is applicable to any IT spending cycle including post-pandemic and AI investment cycles. The structural technology shifts identified (security, EAI, CRM) have all materialized.
prescience_rationale: Aberdeen's prediction that IT spending was at or near its 2002 trough proved accurate: Gartner/CNET data shows IT spending grew 3.7% in 2002 and 9% in 2003. The identified technology priorities (security, EAI/integration, CRM) all received increased investment as spending recovered from 2003 onward.
